coredata.dc:title: Ammonium citrate derived carbon quantum dot as on-off-on fluorescent sensor for detection of chromium(VI) and sulfites
coredata.dc:creator: Fang, Liyang
Zhang, Lu
Chen, Zhenzhen
Zhu, Chaosheng
Liu, Juanjuan
Zheng, Jingtang
coredata.dc:description: The ammonium citrate derived carbon quantum dot (C-dots) with strong fluorescence and high photostability were synthesized via one-step hydrothermal approach. Based on fluorescence quenching and fluorescence recovery of C-dots, the as-prepared C-dots were innovatively developed as an effective on-off-on fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of Cr(VI) and sulfites with detection limit 0.01μmol/L and 0.35μmol/L, respectively. It is found that mechanism of fluorescence quenching of the as-prepared C-dots by Cr(VI) can be mainly attributed to a kind of electron exchange quenching.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ammonium citrate derived C-dots
On-off-on fluorescent probe
Electron exchange quenching
The detections of Cr(VI) and sulfites

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: On-off-on fluorescent probe for the detection of Cr(VI) and sulfites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption spectrum and (b) PL spectra at different excitation wavelengths of CDs-220. (c) Optimal excitation (1) and emission (2) of PL spectra of CDs-220 and the ultraviolet absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) (3). (d) Schematic for electron exchange.

(a) PL intensity of CDs-220 in the presence of various metal ions aqueous solution (λex=350nm). (b) PL response of CDs-220 in the presence of different concentrations of Cr(VI) (λex=350nm). (c) The plot of PL intensity against Cr(VI) concentrations ranging from 0 to 50μM

(a) PL response of C-dots/Cr(VI) mixture to sulfite in the presence of different concentrations of sulfite (λex=350nm pH=2). (b) The plot of PL intensity against sulfite concentrations ranging from 0 to 80μM, (insert) the plot of the fluorescence intensity against the sulfite concentration in the range of 10−50μM

head.ce:title: Ammonium citrate derived carbon quantum dot as on-off-on fluorescent sensor for detection of chromium(VI) and sulfites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, School of Chemical Engineering, China University of Petroleum, Qingdao 266580, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State key laboratory of Molecular Engineering of Polymers, Fudan University, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State key laboratory of Molecular Engineering of Polymers

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, School of Chemical Engineering, China University of Petroleum, Qingdao 266580, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of synthesis of C-dots and fluorescence assays for Cr(VI) and sulfites based on the fluorescence quenching and fluorescence recovery on the C-dots.

The ammonium citrate derived carbon quantum dot (C-dots) with strong fluorescence and high photostability were synthesized via one-step hydrothermal approach. Based on fluorescence quenching and fluorescence recovery of C-dots, the as-prepared C-dots were innovatively developed as an effective on-off-on fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of Cr(VI) and sulfites with detection limit 0.01μmol/L and 0.35μmol/L, respectively. It is found that mechanism of fluorescence quenching of the as-prepared C-dots by Cr(VI) can be mainly attributed to a kind of electron exchange quenching.

ce:keyword.ce:text: The detections of Cr(VI) and sulfites

ce:section.ce:para: The development and design of fluorescence sensors for the detections of chromium(VI) (Cr(VI)) and sulfites are very important and imperative because the toxic pollutant are released into the environment. In recent years, there has been a rapid growth in the use of carbon quantum dots (C-dots)-based fluorescence probe for the detection of molecular, ions and pH due to its good sensitivity and selectivity [1–3]. However, there is little research for the application of C-dots-based fluorescent probes to determine Cr(VI) and sulfites. Particularly, to the best of our knowledge, there are no reports related to the method of the determination of sulfites based on C-dots fluorescence recovery. Thus, C-dots-based fluorescence probe for detection of both Cr(VI) and sulfites is worth desiring.

Herein, the N-doped ammonium citrate derived C-dots were synthesized via facile and economical one-step hydrothermal approach. Ammonium citrate as both carbon source and nitrogen source is good candidate to synthesize N-doped C-dots [4]. The C-dots show strong fluorescence emission, good photochemical stability. Especially, its excitation and emission bands are almost fully overlap with the absorption bands of Cr(IV), which is very beneficial to the electron-exchange between Cr(VI) and C-dots, leading to excellent fluorescence quenching ability of Cr(VI) on the as-prepared C-dots. Based on the fluorescence quenching of Cr(VI), the as-made C-dots were applied as “turn-off” probe for determination of Cr(VI), and its detection limitation is 0.01 μmol/L far below the standard of the World Health Organization (WHO) in which Cr(VI) concentration should be lower than 50μg/L in drinking water [5]. Because the C-dots fluorescence can recover when Cr(VI) is reduced to Cr3+ by sulfite, a “turn-on” probe for detection of sulfites can be developed creatively with the detection limitation 0.35μmol/L. The on-off-on C-dots-based fluorescence probes have good selectivity and high sensitivity for detection of Cr(VI) and sulfites.

Ammonium citrate aqueous solution (0.176mol/L, 70mL) was transferred into a 100mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 220°C for 4h, and cooled down to ambient temperature. Then, purification of the samples was accomplished by centrifuging and filtering. Finally, the obtained C-dots were concentrated, dried and recorded as CDs-220.

A very small amount of Cr(VI) aqueous solution (1×10−4 mol/L) was added stepwise into the 4ml C-dots solution (0.4mg/L) by a microinjector in order to keep the volume of the nearly unchanged. The fluorescence emission spectra of the mixed solution (Cr(VI) concentration ranged from 0 to 50μmol/L) were recorded.

The different volume of sulfites aqueous solution (1×10−3 mol/L) was added into the C-dots/Cr(VI) aqueous solution (Cr(VI) concentration is 5×10−5 mol/L, pH=2) to form mixed solutions in which SO3 2− concentration ranged from 0 to 80μmol/L with a 5μmol/L spacing, respectively. Then, their fluorescence emission spectra were measured.

By the characterization of TEM, XRD, Raman, FT-IR and XPS (Figs. S1 and S2), it is found that the as-prepared C-dots are monodispersed particles of near-spherical shape with average diameter 3nm, and consist of sp2 carbons nanocrystalline core (honeycomb frame) and functionalized surface with plentiful carboxyl, hydroxyl, amino and amide groups (Details are at supporting information (SI)). Therefore, the as-prepared C-dots can be considered as both good electron donors and electron acceptors [6], which is very helpful for probe based on the fluorescence quenching. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs-220 (Fig. 1 a) displays characteristic absorption about 205nm due to the π→π∗ transition of aromatic sp2 domains [7], and a weak shoulder peak at 320nm can be attributed to the n→π∗ transition of CO. The photograph of CDs-220 exhibits yellow brown transparent liquid appearance in daylight and blue color fluorescence under UV light (365nm) (Fig. 1a, inset). Meanwhile, in Fig. 1b, the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of CDs-220 presents that the emission peak shifts from 410 to 490nm as excitation wavelengths increase from 290 to 450nm, and its intensity increases at first and decreases later on. CDs-220 displays the maximum emission peak at 439nm (λex=350nm). The QY of CDs-220 was measured to be as high as 20.05%. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-220 has not changed much under continuous UV irradiation (365nm) for 6h (Fig. S3a), in ambient air at room temperature for 2months (Fig. S3b) in various concentrations of NaCl solution (Fig. S3c) and at pH from 3 to 10 (Fig. S3d), suggesting that CDs-220 has outstanding photochemical stability.

In general, there are at least three mechanisms for quenching [8]: (1) intersystem crossing or the heavy atom effect; (2) electron exchange quenching; (3) photoinduced electron transfer quenching. Fig. 1d shows a schematic for the electron exchange [8]. The electron exchange occurs between a donor and an acceptor. The excited donor has an electron in the LU orbital. This electron transfers to the acceptor. Then an electron of the acceptor from the HO orbital transfers back to the donor, so the acceptor is left in an excited state. In the process, energy is transferred from the donor to the acceptor by electron exchange. This mode of energy transfer depends on spectral overlap of the donor and the acceptor [8]. A high efficiency electron exchange quenching needs a fine spectral overlap between the absorption peak of the quencher and the excitation and/or emission peak of the fluorophore. Obviously, the fluorescence quenching of CDs-220 by Cr(VI) can be attributed to electron exchange quenching. As shown in Fig. 1c, the excitation spectrum of CDs-220 has a band at 354nm, and the emission band of CDs-220 under the excitation of 350nm is centered at 439nm. Cr(VI) exhibits broad absorption at 296, 357 and 440nm, respectively. The absorption bands of Cr(IV) almost fully covered with the excitation and emission bands of CDs-220, which is very beneficial to electron-exchange between Cr(VI) and the C-dots. As a consequence, Cr(VI) can successfully quench fluorescence of the as-prepared C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: On-off-on fluorescent probe for the detection of Cr(VI) and sulfites
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of metallic ions including K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Cd2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− on the fluorescence intensity of CD-220 were investigated. Fig. 2 a exhibits that the fluorescence intensity of CDs-220 is not sensitive to most of the metal ions (at a concentration of 1.0×10−5 mol/L) except Cr(VI), Fe3+ and Cu2+. Particularly, Cr(VI) shows the outstanding fluorescence quenching ability to CDs-220. Meanwhile, Cu2+ and Fe3+ have negligible interference with a relative error within 5.0% for Cr(VI) detection (Detail are at SI), suggesting good selectivity of CDs-220 for Cr(VI).

The response of CDs-220 in the presence of Cr(VI) was determined by titration of the CDs-220 with Cr(VI). Upon addition of Cr(VI) at a 0–50μmol/L concentration, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-220 was quenched by 98% (Fig. 2b). The experiment shows a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of CDs-220 and Cr(VI) concentration with a linear regression equation of ln(F0/F)=0.0775[C]+0.01 and a correlation coefficient of 0.9990 (Fig. 2c). Here F0 and F are fluorescence intensity of CDs-220 in the absence and presence of Cr(VI) respectively, and [C] is the concentration of Cr(VI). The detection limit is 0.01μmol/L is calculated based on a 3Sb/k (Sb is the standard deviation of the blank signal and k is the slope of the linear calibration plot), suggesting that CDs-220 possesses a good sensitivity for the detection of Cr(VI).

For the C-dots-Cr(VI) system, Cr(VI) can be reduced to Cr3+ by sulfite, resulting in the recovery of CDs-220 fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots−Cr(VI)/sulfite mixtures is enhanced gradually with the increase of concentration of sulfite (in the pH=2). The C-dots fluorescence can be recovered to 89.6% when the concentration of sulfite reached 100μmol/L, indicating that the turn-off probe can be used repeatedly. More importantly, a good linear relationship between PL intensity and sulfite concentration is observed from 10 to 50μmol/L, so the C-dots-Cr(VI) system as a “turn-on” fluorescent probe can be used to detect sulfite. Meanwhile, the linear relationship can be described by the linear equations: F/F0 =0.447[C]−3.095 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9973 (Fig. 3b inset). Where F and F0 are PL intensity of C-dots−Cr(VI) mixtures in the presence and absence of sulfite, respectively, and [C] is the sulfite concentration. The detection limit comes to 0.35μmol/L.

In summary, we have synthesized the C-dots with strong fluorescent and high photochemical stability via one-step hydrothermal approach from ammonium citrate. Then we successfully developed a simple, convenient, economical and high-efficient on-off-on fluorescent sensor for determination of Cr(VI) and sulfites based on its fluorescence quenching and fluorescence recovery on the C-dots. The “turn-off” probe demonstrated high selectivity and good sensitive for Cr(VI) in a large concentration range of 0–50μmol/L. The detection limitation is 0.01μmol/L. The “turn-on” probe for sulfites has 0.35μmol/L detection limitation and a linear concentration range of 10–50μmol/L. Therefore, the C-dots-based sensor as a good fluorescence probe candidate have promising application prospect in Cr(VI) and sulfites detections.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21376268) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 15CX08005A).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2016.12.098.
